prompt:
A reviewer praises the pianist’s “nimble” touch, noting how easily she shifts from delicate passages to rapid, forceful ones.
As used in the text, what does “nimble” most nearly mean?

choices.A: hesitant
choices.B: skillful
choices.C: ordinary
choices.D: loud

explanation: In context, “nimble” means agile and skillful in movement or technique.

prompt:
Some transportation agencies have begun planting native grasses and wildflowers along roadsides instead of maintaining large stretches of closely cut turf. These plantings can reduce soil erosion and provide habitat for pollinators. As a result, roadside vegetation is increasingly being managed for ecological benefits as well as appearance.
Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

choices.A: Closely cut turf is easier to maintain than native roadside plantings.
choices.B: Transportation agencies plant wildflowers mainly to make roads more colorful.
choices.C: Native roadside plantings can offer environmental benefits in addition to visual ones.
choices.D: Pollinators depend entirely on grasses planted near roads.

explanation: The text emphasizes erosion control and pollinator habitat as well as appearance.

prompt:
Before precise clocks became widely available, some observatories compared local observations of eclipses and planetary movements with reports sent from other cities. By combining records from multiple locations, astronomers could check whether a pattern they noticed in one place also appeared elsewhere.
Which choice can most reasonably be inferred from the text?

choices.A: Astronomers used shared observations to test whether their local findings held more broadly.
choices.B: Observatories usually worked in complete isolation from one another.
choices.C: Eclipse observations became less accurate when more cities contributed reports.
choices.D: Planetary movements could be measured only in cities with precise clocks.

explanation: The passage says records from multiple locations helped astronomers see whether a pattern appeared elsewhere.

prompt:
A materials scientist points out that tiny bubbles trapped in a ceramic glaze can reveal how hot a vessel became in the kiln. She then explains that archaeologists sometimes study those bubbles to estimate the firing temperatures used by ancient potters.
Which choice best describes the function of the first sentence in the text?

choices.A: It provides background on modern pottery manufacturing.
choices.B: It introduces evidence that the second sentence explains the use of.
choices.C: It contradicts the claim made in the second sentence.
choices.D: It argues that ancient potters preferred low firing temperatures.

explanation: The first sentence presents the bubbles in glaze, and the second explains how archaeologists use them.

prompt:
A student is writing about neighborhood walking tours and has taken these notes:
• Some neighborhood groups organize guided walking tours.
• Guides may use old photographs, maps, and oral histories during the tours.
• The tours can help residents learn about local history.
• They may also encourage conversations among longtime and newer residents.
The student wants to emphasize how these tours can connect people with both the past and one another. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.A: Guided neighborhood walking tours sometimes use old photographs, maps, and oral histories.
choices.B: Some neighborhood groups organize walking tours for local residents.
choices.C: By using historical materials to introduce local history, neighborhood walking tours can also spark conversations among residents from different backgrounds.
choices.D: Residents often enjoy seeing maps during neighborhood events.

explanation: This choice combines both goals named in the notes: learning about the past and encouraging conversation.

prompt:
The farm tested a frost-resistant variety of spinach for two growing seasons. ______, the owner decided to plant more of it the following spring.
Which choice completes the text with the most logical transition?

choices.A: For instance,
choices.B: Consequently,
choices.C: Meanwhile,
choices.D: Instead,

explanation: Planting more spinach is the result of the successful two-season test.

prompt:
The chef’s signature dessert<u>—a pear tart with rosemary syrup—</u>appears on the menu year-round.
Which choice best punctuates the underlined portion of the text?

choices.A: NO CHANGE
choices.B: , a pear tart with rosemary syrup,
choices.C: ; a pear tart with rosemary syrup;
choices.D: : a pear tart with rosemary syrup:

explanation: The pair of dashes correctly sets off a nonessential appositive.

prompt:
The storm ended before dawn, <u>the repair crew did not begin work until the roads were inspected.</u>
Which choice most effectively revises the underlined portion of the text to maintain the grammatical correctness of the sentence?

choices.A: the repair crew did not begin work until the roads were inspected.
choices.B: but the repair crew did not begin work until the roads were inspected.
choices.C: the repair crew not beginning work until the roads were inspected.
choices.D: and, the repair crew did not begin work until the roads were inspected.

explanation: “But” correctly joins the clauses and shows the contrast between the storm ending and the delayed start of repairs.

prompt:
The scholarship was awarded to Maya and <u>he</u> after a final interview with the selection committee.
Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.A: he
choices.B: him
choices.C: his
choices.D: himself

explanation: The pronoun is part of the compound object of the preposition “to,” so the objective case is needed.

prompt:
For the past six months, the restoration team <u>meets</u> every Thursday to clean and repair the mural.
Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.A: meets
choices.B: met
choices.C: has met
choices.D: will meet

explanation: “For the past six months” indicates an action continuing from the past to the present, requiring the present perfect.

prompt:
To submit the application before the deadline, <u>the forms were reviewed twice by Carmen.</u>
Which choice best revises the text to ensure that the modifying phrase is correctly placed?

choices.A: the forms were reviewed twice by Carmen.
choices.B: Carmen reviewed the forms twice.
choices.C: the deadline required the forms to be reviewed twice by Carmen.
choices.D: reviewing the forms twice, Carmen submitted them.

explanation: The revision correctly places Carmen as the person acting in the introductory phrase.

prompt:
[1] Some cities support community refrigerators where residents can leave or take food at no cost. [2] Volunteers often monitor the refrigerators and post guidelines for safe use. [3] These sites can help reduce food waste by redistributing items that might otherwise be discarded. [4] They can also provide quick access to food for neighbors who need it.
Which choice best describes the most logical placement for the following sentence?
“In this way, a single shared appliance can serve both environmental and social goals.”

choices.A: Before sentence 1
choices.B: After sentence 2
choices.C: After sentence 3
choices.D: After sentence 4

explanation: The added sentence summarizes both the environmental benefit in sentence 3 and the social benefit in sentence 4.

prompt:
A teacher recorded the average number of minutes four student groups spent setting up a science experiment.
Group 1: 9
Group 2: 12
Group 3: 8
Group 4: 10
Which choice is best supported by the data?

choices.A: Group 1 spent more time setting up the experiment, on average, than Group 4.
choices.B: Group 2 spent the most time setting up the experiment, on average.
choices.C: Group 3 and Group 4 spent the same average amount of time setting up the experiment.
choices.D: The average setup time increased from Group 1 through Group 4.

explanation: Group 2’s average of 12 minutes is the highest.

prompt:
The editor described the memo as surprisingly <u>subtle</u>, praising the way it suggested changes without sounding harsh or direct.
As used in the text, what does “subtle” most nearly mean?

choices.A: indirect
choices.B: colorful
choices.C: lengthy
choices.D: uncertain

explanation: Here “subtle” means understated or indirect rather than harsh or obvious.

prompt:
In a memoir, a writer recalls her grandfather repairing watches at the kitchen table: “He worked so quietly that the ticking of the clocks seemed louder than his tools. Yet every motion of his hands carried the confidence of long practice.”
Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

choices.A: The writer remembers her grandfather as both calm and highly skilled.
choices.B: The writer found the ticking of the clocks distracting and unpleasant.
choices.C: The grandfather repaired watches only as a hobby and rarely succeeded.
choices.D: The kitchen table was too crowded for the grandfather’s work.

explanation: The quiet work and confident hands together create that impression.

prompt:
In a study of bird-safe glass, researchers found that windows marked with thin visible patterns were associated with fewer bird collisions than unmarked windows at the same building. The researchers suggest that the patterns may make the surface easier for birds to detect.
Which choice is best supported by the text?

choices.A: Birds are unable to see any kind of glass surface.
choices.B: Visible window patterns may help reduce bird collisions.
choices.C: Marked windows are effective only on very tall buildings.
choices.D: The study proved that building height has no effect on bird collisions.

explanation: The study found fewer collisions at marked windows and suggests the patterns helped birds detect the glass.

prompt:
A theater historian writes, “When ticket prices were first printed in newspaper advertisements, readers could begin comparing performances not just by title or cast but by cost.”
Why does the author most likely include the quotation in the text?

choices.A: To explain one historian’s view of how printed prices changed the way readers evaluated performances
choices.B: To prove that newspaper advertisements always increased ticket sales
choices.C: To show that casts were more important than prices to readers
choices.D: To describe how theaters printed their advertisements

explanation: The quotation explains how prices gave readers a new basis for comparison.

prompt:
A student is writing about citizen science rainfall logs and has taken these notes:
• In some places, volunteers measure daily rainfall in backyard gauges.
• The records can supplement data collected by official weather stations.
• Local measurements may help show how rainfall differs across short distances.
• These records can support studies of flooding and drought.
Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to make a general claim about citizen rainfall logs?

choices.A: Citizen rainfall logs can add useful local detail to official weather records and help researchers study patterns related to flooding and drought.
choices.B: Backyard gauges are always more accurate than official weather stations.
choices.C: Rainfall differs in every neighborhood for exactly the same reasons.
choices.D: Only professional meteorologists can measure rainfall reliably.

explanation: This choice accurately synthesizes the notes into a general claim.

prompt:
The essay opens with a description of the town’s train station in 1908. ______, it turns to the station’s renovation in the present day.
Which choice completes the text with the most logical transition?

choices.A: In contrast,
choices.B: Previously,
choices.C: Later,
choices.D: Otherwise,

explanation: The essay moves from an earlier historical moment to the present day.

prompt:
The internship allowed Zoe to scan documents, label photographs, and <u>organizing folders in the digital archive.</u>
Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.A: organizing folders in the digital archive.
choices.B: organize folders in the digital archive.
choices.C: organized folders in the digital archive.
choices.D: to organizing folders in the digital archive.

explanation: “Organize” matches the parallel verbs “scan” and “label.”

prompt:
The artists’ cooperative opened a second studio downtown last year.
Which choice best explains the use of the apostrophe in the underlined word?

choices.A: It shows that more than one artist belongs to the cooperative.
choices.B: It shows that the cooperative belongs to the studio.
choices.C: It marks the plural form of a noncount noun.
choices.D: It is used incorrectly because no possession is shown.

explanation: The apostrophe after the plural noun “artists” indicates shared possession.

prompt:
One of the reasons the proposal <u>were</u> delayed was that the final cost estimate had not been approved.
Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.A: were
choices.B: was
choices.C: be
choices.D: being

explanation: The subject is “One,” which is singular, so the verb must be singular.

prompt:
A student wrote the following sentence in a draft:
“The committee revised the schedule, and this made the program easier to manage.”
Which choice best explains why the sentence should be revised?

choices.A: The sentence should be revised because the pronoun “this” has an unclear antecedent.
choices.B: The sentence should be revised because it contains too many verbs.
choices.C: The sentence should be revised because it should use the future tense.
choices.D: The sentence should be revised because the conjunction “and” is always incorrect.

explanation: It is not fully clear whether “this” refers to revising the schedule, the schedule itself, or the revision process.

prompt:
[1] Some schools have planted shade trees near playgrounds and courtyards. [2] As the trees mature, they can cool paved surfaces by blocking direct sunlight. [3] They may also make outdoor areas more comfortable during warm weather. [4] In some cases, schools use the plantings as part of science lessons about soils, insects, and seasonal change.
Which choice best describes the most logical placement for the following sentence?
“The same project can therefore affect both the physical environment and students’ learning.”

choices.A: Before sentence 1
choices.B: After sentence 2
choices.C: After sentence 3
choices.D: After sentence 4

explanation: The sentence best follows the mention of both cooling effects and science lessons, which it summarizes.

prompt:
To make the sentence more concise, the editor replaced “due to the fact that” with <u>because</u>.
Which choice best describes the effect of the revision?

choices.A: It changes the meaning by making the cause less certain.
choices.B: It makes the sentence shorter without changing its meaning.
choices.C: It makes the sentence less grammatical.
choices.D: It introduces an overly informal phrase.

explanation: “Because” is a more concise equivalent of “due to the fact that.”

prompt:
In an experiment, moths released in a dim room flew more directly toward a light source when a small reference light was placed elsewhere in the room than when the source was presented alone. The researchers suggest that additional visual cues may help the moths orient themselves.
Which choice is best supported by the text?

choices.A: Additional visual cues may influence how directly moths move toward a light source.
choices.B: Moths avoid all light sources in dim rooms.
choices.C: A single light source always gives moths enough information to orient themselves.
choices.D: The reference light caused the moths to stop flying.

explanation: The extra reference light was associated with more direct movement.

prompt:
An article about repair cafés explains that volunteers at these events help people fix small appliances, clothing, and tools instead of throwing them away. The article concludes by noting that some municipalities now partner with repair cafés as part of waste-reduction efforts.
What is the function of the final sentence in the text?

choices.A: It offers an example of how the idea has been adopted more broadly.
choices.B: It argues that municipalities should manage all repair work.
choices.C: It introduces a topic unrelated to waste reduction.
choices.D: It denies that repair cafés help reduce waste.

explanation:
The final sentence shows municipalities using repair cafés in official waste-reduction efforts.
PART D — MODULE 2 ANSWER KEY + EXPLANATIONS
